Test 56: Delivery Route Planner (JavaScript) Score: 6.91 / 14
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vans.

The input JSON contains:
- `depot`: [x, y]
- `truckCount`: maximum number of vans/routes
- `packages`: objects `{id, x, y}`

Return JSON:
```
{"routes": [[packageId, packageId, ...], ...]}
```
Every package must appear exactly once. Each route starts and ends at the depot.
The score compares your total distance to a deterministic reference planner.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.1

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Plan multi-vehicle delivery routes over clustered city coordinates.		 0.7331
[30 packages, 2 vans] distance 4129.1, reference 3027.0, score 0.733 (exact nearest-neighbor grading) (run 0.07s)

Delivery Route Planner - 30 packages, 2 vans

View JavaScript code (1338 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
  const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  const { depot, truckCount, packages } = JSON.parse(input);

  const numTrucks = truckCount;
  const numPackages = packages.length;

  if (numPackages === 0) {
    console.log(JSON.stringify({ routes: Array(numTrucks).fill([]) }));
    return;
  }

  // Calculate distances for quick reference
  const getDist = (p1, p2) => Math.hypot(p1[0] - p2[0], p1[1] - p2[1]);

  // Packages with depot as a reference
  const unvisited = new Set(packages);
  const routes = Array.from({ length: numTrucks }, () => []);
  
  // Current position of each truck (starts at depot)
  const truckPositions = Array.from({ length: numTrucks }, () => depot);

  while (unvisited.size > 0) {
    for (let i = 0; i < numTrucks; i++) {
      if (unvisited.size === 0) break;

      let closestPackage = null;
      let minDistance = Infinity;

      for (const pkg of unvisited) {
        const dist = getDist(truckPositions[i], [pkg.x, pkg.y]);
        if (dist < minDistance) {
          minDistance = dist;
          closestPackage = pkg;
        }
      }

      routes[i].push(closestPackage.id);
      truckPositions[i] = [closestPackage.x, closestPackage.y];
      unvisited.delete(closestPackage);
    }
  }

  console.log(JSON.stringify({ routes }));
}

solve();

Total distance: 4129.1 (reference 3027.0)

Test 57: Wildfire Firebreak Planner (JavaScript) Score: 5.23 / 7
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Choose where to cut firebreaks before a wildfire spreads.

The input JSON contains:
- `width`, `height`
- `grid`: strings where `.` is burnable forest and `~` is water/rock
- `ignitions`: starting fire cells `[x, y]`
- `towns`: objects `{id, x, y, value}`
- `cutBudget`: maximum number of forest cells you may cut

Fire spreads in four directions through burnable cells. Water/rock and your cut
cells block fire. Return JSON:
```
{"firebreaks": [[x, y], [x, y], ...]}
```
The score is the fraction of otherwise-burned town value saved.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.1

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.
 Prompt Changes:
Prompt parameters change between subpasses (increasing difficulty)
Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt
View raw AI output
View chain of thought
Choose firebreak cells on a forest map to protect towns from spreading fire.		 0.8000
[32x22 forest, 3 towns] town value burned 0/27, burned cells 1/663, valid cuts 8 (run 0.07s)

Wildfire Firebreak Planner - 32x22 forest, 3 towns

View JavaScript code (2330 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
    const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const { width, height, grid, ignitions, towns, cutBudget } = JSON.parse(input);

    // Represent grid as a 2D array for easier access
    const forest = grid.map(row => row.split(''));

    // BFS to find the distance of each cell from the nearest ignition
    const distances = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(Infinity));
    const queue = [];

    for (const [x, y] of ignitions) {
        if (forest[y][x] === '.') {
            distances[y][x] = 0;
            queue.push([x, y]);
        }
    }

    let head = 0;
    while (head < queue.length) {
        const [x, y] = queue[head++];
        const d = distances[y][x];

        const neighbors = [
            [x + 1, y], [x - 1, y], [x, y + 1], [x, y - 1]
        ];

        for (const [nx, ny] of neighbors) {
            if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height &&
                forest[ny][nx] === '.' && distances[ny][nx] === Infinity) {
                distances[ny][nx] = d + 1;
                queue.push([nx, ny]);
            }
        }
    }

    // Identify candidate cut cells: burnable cells that are on the shortest path
    // between fire and towns. Simple heuristic: prioritize cells with low distance
    // from fire that are close to high-value towns.
    const candidates = [];
    for (let y = 0; y < height; y++) {
        for (let x = 0; x < width; x++) {
            if (forest[y][x] === '.' && distances[y][x] !== Infinity) {
                let townPotential = 0;
                for (const town of towns) {
                    const distToTown = Math.abs(town.x - x) + Math.abs(town.y - y);
                    // Heuristic: cells closer to fire AND closer to towns are high priority
                    // The idea is to cut them off early.
                    townPotential += town.value / (distToTown + 1);
                }
                candidates.push({ x, y, score: townPotential / (distances[y][x] + 1) });
            }
        }
    }

    // Sort by score descending and take the top cutBudget cells
    candidates.sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score);
    const firebreaks = candidates.slice(0, cutBudget).map(c => [c.x, c.y]);

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ firebreaks }));
}

solve();

Test 58: Warehouse Robot Job Planner (JavaScript) Score: 0.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots.

The input JSON contains:
- `grid`: strings where `.` is open floor and `#` is blocked shelving
- `robots`: objects `{id, x, y}`
- `jobs`: objects `{id, x, y, priority}`

Return JSON:
```
{"assignments": [[jobId, jobId, ...], ...]}
```
The array index is the robot id. Every job must appear exactly once. The grader
uses shortest grid walking distance through open cells and compares total travel
to a deterministic greedy reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots moving through aisles and blocked shelves.		 0.0000
Assignments must include every job exactly once; got 16 visits (run 0.25s)

Warehouse Robot Job Planner - 28 jobs, 2 robots

View JavaScript code (2801 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

/**
 * Calculates the shortest path distance between two points in a grid using BFS.
 * @param {string[]} grid 
 * @param {number} startX 
 * @param {number} startY 
 * @param {number} targetX 
 * @param {number} targetY 
 * @returns {number} Distance, or Infinity if unreachable.
 */
function getDistance(grid, startX, startY, targetX, targetY) {
    if (startX === targetX && startY === targetY) return 0;
    const rows = grid.length;
    const cols = grid[0].length;
    const queue = [[startX, startY, 0]];
    const visited = new Set();
    visited.add(`${startX},${startY}`);

    while (queue.length > 0) {
        const [x, y, dist] = queue.shift();

        const neighbors = [
            [x + 1, y], [x - 1, y], [x, y + 1], [x, y - 1]
        ];

        for (const [nx, ny] of neighbors) {
            if (nx >= 0 && nx < rows && ny >= 0 && ny < cols &&
                grid[nx][ny] === '.' && !visited.has(`${nx},${ny}`)) {
                
                if (nx === targetX && ny === targetY) return dist + 1;
                
                visited.add(`${nx},${ny}`);
                queue.push([nx, ny, dist + 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return Infinity;
}

function solve() {
    const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const { grid, robots, jobs } = JSON.parse(input);

    const numRobots = robots.length;
    const assignments = Array.from({ length: numRobots }, () => []);
    
    // Create a working list of jobs
    let remainingJobs = jobs.map(job => ({...job}));

    // Simple greedy approach:
    // While jobs remain, find the robot that can reach a job with the shortest distance,
    // prioritizing higher priority jobs if possible.
    
    while (remainingJobs.length > 0) {
        let bestPair = null;
        let minDistance = Infinity;

        for (let i = 0; i < numRobots; i++) {
            const robot = robots[i];
            for (let j = 0; j < remainingJobs.length; j++) {
                const job = remainingJobs[j];
                const dist = getDistance(grid, robot.x, robot.y, job.x, job.y);
                
                // Heuristic: Prefer shorter distance, then higher priority
                if (dist < minDistance || (dist === minDistance && bestPair && job.priority > remainingJobs[bestPair.jobIdx].priority)) {
                    minDistance = dist;
                    bestPair = { robotIdx: i, jobIdx: j };
                }
            }
        }

        if (bestPair) {
            assignments[bestPair.robotIdx].push(remainingJobs[bestPair.jobIdx].id);
            remainingJobs.splice(bestPair.jobIdx, 1);
        } else {
            // Should not happen if all jobs are reachable
            break;
        }
    }

    console.log(JSON.stringify({ assignments }));
}

solve();

Test 59: Drone Search Coverage Planner (JavaScript) Score: 0.18 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan grid paths for search drones.

The input JSON contains:
- `probability`: 2D array; -1 is an obstacle, positive values are search value
- `drones`: starting cells `{id, x, y}`
- `maxStepsPerDrone`

Return JSON:
```
{"paths": [[[x,y], [x,y], ...], ...]}
```
Each path belongs to the drone with the same index. Moves must be up/down/left/right
or stay in place, must stay inside the grid, and must avoid obstacles. The score is
unique probability covered compared with a greedy reference search.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Architecture: AMD64

Core count: 64

Route search drones over a probability heatmap with obstacles and battery limits.		 0.1832
[2 drones, 55 steps] coverage 2651, reference 14473, score 0.183 (run 0.06s)

Drone Search Coverage Planner - 2 drones, 55 steps

View JavaScript code (1658 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
    const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const data = JSON.parse(input);

    const { probability, drones, maxStepsPerDrone } = data;
    const height = probability.length;
    const width = probability[0].length;

    const paths = [];

    // Basic greedy algorithm: each drone moves to the highest value neighbor 
    // that hasn't been visited by another drone in this step, or stays if no better move.
    // For simplicity, we just have each drone try to maximize its own coverage.

    for (const drone of drones) {
        const path = [[drone.x, drone.y]];
        let currX = drone.x;
        let currY = drone.y;

        for (let step = 0; step < maxStepsPerDrone; step++) {
            let bestVal = -1;
            let bestMove = [currX, currY];

            // Possible moves: stay, up, down, left, right
            const moves = [
                [0, 0], [-1, 0], [1, 0], [0, -1], [0, 1]
            ];

            for (const [dx, dy] of moves) {
                const nextX = currX + dx;
                const nextY = currY + dy;

                if (nextX >= 0 && nextX < height && nextY >= 0 && nextY < width) {
                    const val = probability[nextX][nextY];
                    if (val >= 0 && val > bestVal) {
                        bestVal = val;
                        bestMove = [nextX, nextY];
                    }
                }
            }

            currX = bestMove[0];
            currY = bestMove[1];
            path.push([currX, currY]);
        }
        paths.push(path);
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ paths }));
}

solve();

Test 60: Space Mission Work Schedule (JavaScript) Score: 3.99 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js JavaScript program for this benchmark task.

Schedule mission-control work across parallel workstations.

The input JSON contains:
- `machineCount`
- `tasks`: objects `{id, duration, deps, deadline, weight}`

A task may start only after every dependency has finished. Return either:
```
{"schedule": [{"id": taskId, "start": integerTime, "machine": machineIndex}, ...]}
```
or a simpler topological order:
```
{"order": [taskId, taskId, ...]}
```
If you return only an order, the grader will place tasks greedily in that order.
The score compares weighted lateness plus makespan against a deterministic list
scheduler reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
const fs = require('fs'); const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable JavaScript source code.

Subpass 0
Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

Schedule dependent mission tasks across parallel workstations with deadlines.		 0.6291
[24 tasks, 3 workstations] cost 582.3, makespan 106; reference 366.3, makespan 86; score 0.629 (run 0.06s)

Space Mission Work Schedule - 24 tasks, 3 workstations

View JavaScript code (1583 chars)
const fs = require('fs');

function solve() {
  const input = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  if (!input) return;
  const data = JSON.parse(input);
  
  const { machineCount, tasks } = data;
  
  // Calculate dependencies
  const taskMap = new Map();
  tasks.forEach(task => taskMap.set(task.id, task));
  
  // Topological sort / Priority heuristic
  // Simple heuristic: Latest finish time / Critical path
  // For now, let's do a simple dependency-aware ordering based on weights and deadlines.
  
  const inDegree = new Map();
  const adj = new Map();
  
  tasks.forEach(task => {
    inDegree.set(task.id, 0);
    adj.set(task.id, []);
  });
  
  tasks.forEach(task => {
    task.deps.forEach(depId => {
      adj.get(depId).push(task.id);
      inDegree.set(task.id, inDegree.get(task.id) + 1);
    });
  });
  
  const queue = [];
  tasks.forEach(task => {
    if (inDegree.get(task.id) === 0) {
      queue.push(task);
    }
  });
  
  // Priority: prioritize based on deadline and weight (EDF/Weighted)
  const order = [];
  while (queue.length > 0) {
    queue.sort((a, b) => {
      // Heuristic: prioritize sooner deadline, then higher weight
      if (a.deadline !== b.deadline) return a.deadline - b.deadline;
      return b.weight - a.weight;
    });
    
    const task = queue.shift();
    order.push(task.id);
    
    adj.get(task.id).forEach(nextId => {
      inDegree.set(nextId, inDegree.get(nextId) - 1);
      if (inDegree.get(nextId) === 0) {
        queue.push(taskMap.get(nextId));
      }
    });
  }
  
  console.log(JSON.stringify({ order }));
}

solve();

Test 61: Delivery Route Planner (TypeScript) Score: 8.91 / 14
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan delivery routes for a fleet of vans.

The input JSON contains:
- `depot`: [x, y]
- `truckCount`: maximum number of vans/routes
- `packages`: objects `{id, x, y}`

Return JSON:
```
{"routes": [[packageId, packageId, ...], ...]}
```
Every package must appear exactly once. Each route starts and ends at the depot.
The score compares your total distance to a deterministic reference planner.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

Plan multi-vehicle delivery routes over clustered city coordinates.		 0.7331
[30 packages, 2 vans] distance 4129.1, reference 3027.0, score 0.733 (exact nearest-neighbor grading) (run 0.07s)

Delivery Route Planner - 30 packages, 2 vans

View TypeScript code (1803 chars)
declare const require: any;
const fs = require('fs');

interface Point {
    x: number;
    y: number;
}

interface Package {
    id: number;
    x: number;
    y: number;
}

interface InputData {
    depot: [number, number];
    truckCount: number;
    packages: Package[];
}

function distance(p1: Point, p2: Point): number {
    return Math.sqrt(Math.pow(p1.x - p2.x, 2) + Math.pow(p1.y - p2.y, 2));
}

function solve(): void {
    const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const data: InputData = JSON.parse(input);

    const depot: Point = { x: data.depot[0], y: data.depot[1] };
    const packages = [...data.packages];
    const routes: number[][] = Array.from({ length: data.truckCount }, () => []);

    // Greedy approach: assign each package to the truck that makes the most sense.
    // To simplify: distribute packages cyclically or by proximity.
    // Let's use a simple approach: assign to the truck that adds the least distance.

    const currentPositions: Point[] = Array.from({ length: data.truckCount }, () => ({ ...depot }));

    while (packages.length > 0) {
        for (let t = 0; t < data.truckCount; t++) {
            if (packages.length === 0) break;

            let bestPackageIndex = -1;
            let minDistance = Infinity;

            for (let i = 0; i < packages.length; i++) {
                const dist = distance(currentPositions[t], packages[i]);
                if (dist < minDistance) {
                    minDistance = dist;
                    bestPackageIndex = i;
                }
            }

            const pkg = packages.splice(bestPackageIndex, 1)[0];
            routes[t].push(pkg.id);
            currentPositions[t] = { x: pkg.x, y: pkg.y };
        }
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ routes }));
}

solve();

Test 62: Wildfire Firebreak Planner (TypeScript) Score: 0.00 / 7
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Choose where to cut firebreaks before a wildfire spreads.

The input JSON contains:
- `width`, `height`
- `grid`: strings where `.` is burnable forest and `~` is water/rock
- `ignitions`: starting fire cells `[x, y]`
- `towns`: objects `{id, x, y, value}`
- `cutBudget`: maximum number of forest cells you may cut

Fire spreads in four directions through burnable cells. Water/rock and your cut
cells block fire. Return JSON:
```
{"firebreaks": [[x, y], [x, y], ...]}
```
The score is the fraction of otherwise-burned town value saved.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB
SIMD: AVX, AVX2, SSE, SSE2, SSE3, SSE4.1, SSE4.2, SSSE3
SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes

Return the complete runnable TypeScript source code.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

View chain of thought
Choose firebreak cells on a forest map to protect towns from spreading fire.		 0.0000
[32x22 forest, 3 towns] town value burned 27/27, burned cells 658/663, valid cuts 5 (run 0.06s)

Wildfire Firebreak Planner - 32x22 forest, 3 towns

View TypeScript code (3409 chars)
import * as fs from 'fs';

interface Town {
  id: string;
  x: number;
  y: number;
  value: number;
}

interface InputData {
  width: number;
  height: number;
  grid: string[];
  ignitions: [number, number][];
  towns: Town[];
  cutBudget: number;
}

function solve() {
  const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  const data: InputData = JSON.parse(input);

  const { width, height, grid, ignitions, towns, cutBudget } = data;

  // Simple heuristic: 
  // 1. Run BFS/Dijkstra to determine which forest cells ('.') 
  //    are on the path from ignitions to towns.
  // 2. Prioritize cutting cells that are on paths to the most valuable towns.
  
  // This is a simplified approach, but should be a good starting point.
  
  const firebreaks: [number, number][] = [];
  let budgetUsed = 0;

  // Simple heuristic:
  // For each town, find the shortest path from *any* ignition point using BFS.
  // The cells on this path are candidates for cutting.
  // We prioritize towns with high value and reachable paths.

  // 1. Map grid for easy access
  const gridCells = grid.map(row => row.split(''));

  // 2. Simple BFS to get distance from ignitions to all cells
  const dist = Array.from({ length: height }, () => Array(width).fill(Infinity));
  const queue: [number, number, number][] = []; // [x, y, d]
  for (const [ix, iy] of ignitions) {
    if (gridCells[iy][ix] === '.') {
      dist[iy][ix] = 0;
      queue.push([ix, iy, 0]);
    }
  }

  const dx = [0, 0, 1, -1];
  const dy = [1, -1, 0, 0];

  while (queue.length > 0) {
    const [x, y, d] = queue.shift()!;
    for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
      const nx = x + dx[i];
      const ny = y + dy[i];
      if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && gridCells[ny][nx] === '.' && dist[ny][nx] === Infinity) {
        dist[ny][nx] = d + 1;
        queue.push([nx, ny, d + 1]);
      }
    }
  }

  // 3. For each town, find path from closest ignited cell and add cells to candidates
  const candidates: {x: number, y: number, townValue: number, dist: number}[] = [];
  for (const town of towns) {
      // Find path from town back to ignition
      let currX = town.x;
      let currY = town.y;
      
      while (dist[currY][currX] > 0) {
          let bestD = dist[currY][currX];
          let nextX = currX;
          let nextY = currY;
          
          for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
              const nx = currX + dx[i];
              const ny = currY + dy[i];
              if (nx >= 0 && nx < width && ny >= 0 && ny < height && dist[ny][nx] < bestD) {
                  bestD = dist[ny][nx];
                  nextX = nx;
                  nextY = ny;
              }
          }
          
          if (nextX === currX && nextY === currY) break; // Path blocked or no path
          
          currX = nextX;
          currY = nextY;
          
          if (gridCells[currY][currX] === '.') {
              candidates.push({x: currX, y: currY, townValue: town.value, dist: dist[currY][currX]});
          }
      }
  }
  
  // 4. Sort candidates: prioritize high value/dist, cut
  candidates.sort((a, b) => (b.townValue / (b.dist + 1)) - (a.townValue / (a.dist + 1)));

  for (const cand of candidates) {
      if (budgetUsed < cutBudget) {
          firebreaks.push([cand.x, cand.y]);
          budgetUsed++;
      } else {
          break;
      }
  }
  console.log(JSON.stringify({ firebreaks }));
}

solve();

Test 63: Warehouse Robot Job Planner (TypeScript) Score: 1.65 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots.

The input JSON contains:
- `grid`: strings where `.` is open floor and `#` is blocked shelving
- `robots`: objects `{id, x, y}`
- `jobs`: objects `{id, x, y, priority}`

Return JSON:
```
{"assignments": [[jobId, jobId, ...], ...]}
```
The array index is the robot id. Every job must appear exactly once. The grader
uses shortest grid walking distance through open cells and compares total travel
to a deterministic greedy reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**

Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

Assign warehouse pick jobs to robots moving through aisles and blocked shelves.		 0.9073
[28 jobs, 2 robots] travel 151, reference 137, score 0.907 (exact grid grading) (run 0.46s)

Warehouse Robot Job Planner - 28 jobs, 2 robots

View TypeScript code (3007 chars)
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
type Robot = { id: number; x: number; y: number };
type Job = { id: number; x: number; y: number; priority: number };
type Input = { grid: string[]; robots: Robot[]; jobs: Job[] };

function getShortestDistance(
    grid: string[],
    start: Point,
    end: Point
): number {
    const rows = grid.length;
    const cols = grid[0].length;
    const queue: [Point, number][] = [[start, 0]];
    const visited = new Set<string>();
    visited.add(`${start.x},${start.y}`);

    while (queue.length > 0) {
        const [{ x, y }, dist] = queue.shift()!;
        if (x === end.x && y === end.y) return dist;

        const neighbors = [
            { x: x + 1, y },
            { x: x - 1, y },
            { x, y: y + 1 },
            { x, y: y - 1 },
        ];

        for (const next of neighbors) {
            if (
                next.x >= 0 && next.x < cols &&
                next.y >= 0 && next.y < rows &&
                grid[next.y][next.x] !== '#' &&
                !visited.has(`${next.x},${next.y}`)
            ) {
                visited.add(`${next.x},${next.y}`);
                queue.push([next, dist + 1]);
            }
        }
    }
    return Infinity; // Should not happen based on problem constraints
}

function solve() {
    const fs = require('fs');
    const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const data: Input = JSON.parse(input);

    const { grid, robots, jobs } = data;
    const numRobots = robots.length;
    const assignments: number[][] = Array.from({ length: numRobots }, () => []);

    // Simple greedy assignment:
    // Calculate distances from all robots to all jobs
    // Repeatedly assign the closest job-robot pair
    
    let unassignedJobs = [...jobs];
    
    while (unassignedJobs.length > 0) {
        let bestDist = Infinity;
        let bestRobotIdx = -1;
        let bestJobIdx = -1;

        for (let r = 0; r < numRobots; r++) {
            const robot = robots[r];
            // Current position of robot is last assigned job, or initial pos
            const currentPos = assignments[r].length > 0
                ? jobs.find(j => j.id === assignments[r][assignments[r].length - 1])!
                : robot;

            for (let j = 0; j < unassignedJobs.length; j++) {
                const job = unassignedJobs[j];
                const dist = getShortestDistance(grid, { x: currentPos.x, y: currentPos.y }, { x: job.x, y: job.y });
                
                // Heuristic: divide distance by priority to prefer higher priority
                const score = dist / job.priority;

                if (score < bestDist) {
                    bestDist = score;
                    bestRobotIdx = r;
                    bestJobIdx = j;
                }
            }
        }

        const jobToAssign = unassignedJobs.splice(bestJobIdx, 1)[0];
        assignments[bestRobotIdx].push(jobToAssign.id);
    }

    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ assignments }));
}

solve();

Test 64: Drone Search Coverage Planner (TypeScript) Score: 6.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Plan grid paths for search drones.

The input JSON contains:
- `probability`: 2D array; -1 is an obstacle, positive values are search value
- `drones`: starting cells `{id, x, y}`
- `maxStepsPerDrone`

Return JSON:
```
{"paths": [[[x,y], [x,y], ...], ...]}
```
Each path belongs to the drone with the same index. Moves must be up/down/left/right
or stay in place, must stay inside the grid, and must avoid obstacles. The score is
unique probability covered compared with a greedy reference search.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

Route search drones over a probability heatmap with obstacles and battery limits.		 1.0000
[2 drones, 55 steps] coverage 14678, reference 14473, score 1.000 (run 0.29s)

Drone Search Coverage Planner - 2 drones, 55 steps

View TypeScript code (2068 chars)
import * as fs from 'fs';

interface Input {
    probability: number[][];
    drones: { id: number; x: number; y: number }[];
    maxStepsPerDrone: number;
}

interface Output {
    paths: [number, number][][];
}

function solve() {
    const inputString = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
    const data: Input = JSON.parse(inputString);

    const { probability, drones, maxStepsPerDrone } = data;
    const rows = probability.length;
    const cols = probability[0].length;

    // Use a set to track visited high-value cells to avoid redundant coverage
    const visited = new Set<string>();

    const paths: [number, number][][] = drones.map(drone => {
        let { x, y } = drone;
        const path: [number, number][] = [[x, y]];
        visited.add(`${x},${y}`);

        for (let step = 0; step < maxStepsPerDrone; step++) {
            // Greedy strategy: find best next cell
            let bestNext: [number, number] = [x, y];
            let bestValue = -1;

            const moves = [
                [0, 1], [0, -1], [1, 0], [-1, 0], [0, 0] // include stay
            ];

            for (const [dx, dy] of moves) {
                const nx = x + dx;
                const ny = y + dy;

                if (nx >= 0 && nx < cols && ny >= 0 && ny < rows && probability[ny][nx] !== -1) {
                    const key = `${nx},${ny}`;
                    let value = probability[ny][nx];
                    
                    // If already visited, value is 0 (don't want to re-cover if possible)
                    if (visited.has(key)) {
                        value = 0;
                    }

                    if (value > bestValue) {
                        bestValue = value;
                        bestNext = [nx, ny];
                    }
                }
            }

            x = bestNext[0];
            y = bestNext[1];
            path.push([x, y]);
            visited.add(`${x},${y}`);
        }
        return path;
    });

    const output: Output = { paths };
    process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(output));
}

solve();

Test 65: Space Mission Work Schedule (TypeScript) Score: 4.00 / 6
Typical Prompt:
Write a complete Node.js TypeScript program for this benchmark task.

Schedule mission-control work across parallel workstations.

The input JSON contains:
- `machineCount`
- `tasks`: objects `{id, duration, deps, deadline, weight}`

A task may start only after every dependency has finished. Return either:
```
{"schedule": [{"id": taskId, "start": integerTime, "machine": machineIndex}, ...]}
```
or a simpler topological order:
```
{"order": [taskId, taskId, ...]}
```
If you return only an order, the grader will place tasks greedily in that order.
The score compares weighted lateness plus makespan against a deterministic list
scheduler reference.

**Input format:**
The program receives one JSON document on stdin. Use this pattern to read it:
```
declare const require: any; const fs = require('fs'); const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
const data = JSON.parse(input);
```

**Output format:**
Print one JSON document to stdout. Do not print logs or explanatory text.

**Runtime environment:**
Platform: Windows 11 (build 10.0.26200)
Architecture: AMD64
CPU: AMD64 Family 23 Model 8 Stepping 2, AuthenticAMD
Core count: 64
Memory: 127.87 GB

SIMD detail: AVX=yes, AVX-512F=no, AVX2=yes, SSE=yes, SSE2=yes, SSE3=yes, SSE4.1=yes, SSE4.2=yes, SSSE3=yes
Node.js: v24.14.1
TypeScript compiler: Version 6.0.3

The grader will run the same program on increasingly large scenarios. Use algorithms
that scale; avoid browser APIs, network access, and external npm dependencies.
Return the complete runnable TypeScript source code.

Parameters: (see typical prompt)View exact prompt

Schedule dependent mission tasks across parallel workstations with deadlines.		 0.6487
[24 tasks, 3 workstations] cost 564.7, makespan 114; reference 366.3, makespan 86; score 0.649 (run 0.30s)

Space Mission Work Schedule - 24 tasks, 3 workstations

View TypeScript code (3089 chars)
interface Task {
  id: string;
  duration: number;
  deps: string[];
  deadline: number;
  weight: number;
}

interface Input {
  machineCount: number;
  tasks: Task[];
}

interface ScheduleEntry {
  id: string;
  start: number;
  machine: number;
}

function solve() {
  const fs = require('fs');
  const input: string = fs.readFileSync(0, 'utf8');
  const data: Input = JSON.parse(input);

  const { machineCount, tasks } = data;

  // Build graph
  const adj = new Map<string, string[]>();
  const inDegree = new Map<string, number>();
  const taskMap = new Map<string, Task>();
  
  for (const task of tasks) {
    taskMap.set(task.id, task);
    if (!adj.has(task.id)) adj.set(task.id, []);
    if (!inDegree.has(task.id)) inDegree.set(task.id, 0);
  }

  for (const task of tasks) {
    for (const dep of task.deps) {
      if (!adj.has(dep)) adj.set(dep, []);
      adj.get(dep)!.push(task.id);
      inDegree.set(task.id, (inDegree.get(task.id) || 0) + 1);
    }
  }

  // Priority Queue for scheduling based on deadline and weight
  // Simple heuristic: earlier deadline is more urgent
  const readyTasks: Task[] = [];
  for (const [id, degree] of inDegree.entries()) {
    if (degree === 0) {
      readyTasks.push(taskMap.get(id)!);
    }
  }

  const schedule: ScheduleEntry[] = [];
  const machineAvailableTime = new Array(machineCount).fill(0);
  const taskFinishTime = new Map<string, number>();
  const completedTasks = new Set<string>();

  while (readyTasks.length > 0) {
    // Sort ready tasks: earliest deadline first
    readyTasks.sort((a, b) => a.deadline - b.deadline);
    
    const task = readyTasks.shift()!;
    
    // Find earliest start time based on dependencies
    let startTime = 0;
    for (const dep of task.deps) {
      startTime = Math.max(startTime, taskFinishTime.get(dep) || 0);
    }

    // Find earliest available machine
    let machineIdx = 0;
    let minAvailableTime = Infinity;
    
    // Actually, we want to find the machine that becomes free soonest,
    // but the task cannot start before dependencies finish.
    // So the task can start at max(machineAvailableTime[i], dependencyReadyTime).
    
    let bestMachine = 0;
    let bestStartTime = Infinity;

    for(let i=0; i<machineCount; i++) {
        const potentialStartTime = Math.max(startTime, machineAvailableTime[i]);
        if(potentialStartTime < bestStartTime) {
            bestStartTime = potentialStartTime;
            bestMachine = i;
        }
    }

    const finishTime = bestStartTime + task.duration;
    
    schedule.push({
      id: task.id,
      start: bestStartTime,
      machine: bestMachine
    });

    machineAvailableTime[bestMachine] = finishTime;
    taskFinishTime.set(task.id, finishTime);
    completedTasks.add(task.id);

    // Update ready tasks
    for (const neighbor of (adj.get(task.id) || [])) {
      inDegree.set(neighbor, inDegree.get(neighbor)! - 1);
      if (inDegree.get(neighbor) === 0) {
        readyTasks.push(taskMap.get(neighbor)!);
      }
    }
  }

  process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify({ schedule }));
}

solve();
